_note: Lore-back copy for build_metro_cards.py. credit strings are VERBATIM from DIAGRAM-LEDGER.md. facts are curated from the gate-3 almanac research (sources in almanac.json). No em dashes anywhere.

flavor.tokyo: Thirteen lines, two operators, one grid.
flavor.seoul: 146 km between its furthest stations, the deck's longest reach.
flavor.singapore: Six lines, four of them driverless, none older than 1987.
flavor.hong kong: Fares cover the costs and then some: the system runs at a profit.
flavor.paris: 1.35 stations per square km, the deck's tightest grid.
flavor.shanghai: From first train to 800 route-km inside three decades.
flavor.beijing: 909 route-km under one operator, the deck's biggest single system.
flavor.london: Underground since 1863, before anyone else tried.
flavor.nyc: 24 hours a day, every day of the year.
flavor.madrid: 172 km of new metro in sixteen years, Europe's great expansion.
flavor.moscow: Marble, mosaics and a train every 90 seconds.
flavor.copenhagen: Four lines, zero drivers, all night.
flavor.delhi: 374 route-km in barely two decades, and it pays its own way.
flavor.guangzhou: From a 5 km starter line in 1997 to 780 km today.
flavor.mexico city: A pictogram for every station, a 5-peso fare for every ride.
flavor.cairo: Africa’s first metro, opened 1987, still its workhorse.
flavor.osaka: Japan’s oldest municipal subway, anchored by the packed Midosuji spine.
flavor.istanbul: A metro on two continents, and one of the world’s fastest-growing.

tokyo: Two operators share one grid: Tokyo Metro runs nine lines, Toei runs four.
G is for Ginza: Asia’s first metro line, opened 1927.
seoul: Line 2 is a 48 km loop around the city; trains circle it all day.
Every station on the network has platform screen doors; the rollout finished by 2017.
singapore: Many MRT stations double as civil defence shelters, built blast resistant.
The North East Line opened in 2003 as one of the world’s first fully automated heavy metro lines.
hong kong: The rail plus property model funds new lines with real estate built above the stations.
The East Rail corridor dates to 1910, nearly seventy years older than the MTR itself.
paris: Opened for the 1900 World’s Fair; Line 1 still runs the route it opened with.
86 of Guimard’s Art Nouveau entrances from the early 1900s still stand.
shanghai: Set a one-day record of 13.39 million rides on 9 March 2024.
Five lines run driverless, about 169 km of unattended metro.
beijing: China’s first subway, built as a Cold War defence project; Zhou Enlai rode the first train in 1969.
Early tickets required an introduction letter from your work unit.
london: Despite the name, only about 45 percent of the network is below ground.
Tube stations doubled as air-raid shelters during the Blitz.
nyc: Express tracks let fast trains overtake locals, a luxury most metros never built.
Its 472 stations are the most of any single-operator metro in the world.
madrid: Between 1995 and 2011 the network added 172 km and 132 stations, over 10 km a year.
Chamberí station, bypassed in the 1960s, survives as a time-capsule museum of the 1919 metro.
moscow: The Stalin-era stations were built as palaces for the people, in marble and chandeliers.
In 2023 Moscow cut rush-hour intervals to 80 seconds, a world first.
copenhagen: Every train has run driverless since the first one in 2002.
One of only three metros in the world running 24/7, with New York and the Chicago L.
delhi: The first metro in the world to earn UN carbon credits, in 2011.
India’s first driverless trains entered service on the Magenta Line in 2020.
guangzhou: Lines 18 and 22 run at 160 km/h, the fastest metro service in China.
The Guangfo line, opened 2010, was China’s first intercity metro.
mexico city: Every station has its own pictogram, a 1969 design for navigating without reading.
Pino Suárez station holds a small Aztec temple uncovered during construction.
cairo: Africa’s and the Middle East’s first metro, opened 1987.
Trains have carried women-only cars since 1989.
osaka: The Midosuji Line opened in 1933 as Japan’s first municipally run subway.
The New Tram has run as a driverless people-mover since 1981.
istanbul: The M5, opened 2017, was Turkey’s first driverless metro line.
Its lines run on both the European and Asian sides of the Bosphorus.

caveat.delhi: diagram dated 2020; Phase-4 openings not yet drawn
caveat.mexico city: diagram dated 2023, drawn to the sections then in service
caveat.nyc: the Tauranac-form diagram, the city’s map for 45 years
caveat.osaka: diagram c.2011 in the pre-2018 Municipal Subway idiom; the 2024 and 2025 extensions not drawn
